meta.title: Pregnancy BMI Calculator — Pre-Pregnancy BMI by IOM 2009
meta.description: Calculate your pre-pregnancy BMI and classify it into IOM 2009 categories. Includes recommended weight gain for singletons and twins. Free, no sign-up.

pregnancyBMI.title: Pregnancy BMI Calculator
pregnancyBMI.description: Calculate your pre-pregnancy BMI by WHO classification and see recommended weight gain per IOM 2009.
pregnancyBMI.underweightDesc: Low pre-pregnancy weight. Higher gain recommended to support birth weight.
pregnancyBMI.normalDesc: Healthy starting BMI. Moderate gain reduces risks for mother and baby.
pregnancyBMI.overweightDesc: Elevated starting BMI. Lower gain recommended to limit complications.
pregnancyBMI.obeseDesc: High starting BMI. Lower gain reduces risk of gestational diabetes and preeclampsia.
pregnancyBMI.howItWorksText: BMI uses the standard formula: weight (kg) ÷ height² (m). Use your weight BEFORE pregnancy. Weight-gain ranges follow the Institute of Medicine (IOM 2009) guidelines, the global standard. Conditions like gestational diabetes may shift individual targets.
pregnancyBMI.source: Source: Institute of Medicine (IOM, 2009) — Weight Gain During Pregnancy

faq.q: Which weight should I use for pregnancy BMI?
faq.a: Your weight BEFORE pregnancy. That number sets the recommended total gain per IOM 2009. Current pregnancy weight is not used here.
It drives the recommended gain. Underweight women should gain more, women with obesity less — both reduce risks of preterm birth, gestational diabetes, and preeclampsia.

faq.q: What if my BMI sits on a boundary?
faq.a: If your BMI is close to a category cutoff (e.g. 24.8 or 25.2), talk to your OB/GYN. Muscle mass, history, and individual risks can move the target.

faq.q: Is the IOM 2009 guideline used worldwide?
faq.a: Yes. IOM 2009 is the global reference and uses WHO BMI categories. National guidelines (NICE, ACOG, DGGG) align with these ranges.

faq.q: How does it differ for twins?
faq.a: BMI categories are the same. Total recommended gain is higher: 17–25 kg (normal), 14–23 kg (overweight), 11–19 kg (obese). Underweight twins use the singleton range.

faq.q: My BMI is over 30 — what does that mean?
faq.a: Pregnancy with obesity carries higher risks (gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, cesarean). A modest gain of 5–9 kg reduces those risks. Closer prenatal monitoring is advised.
